edges.description: Immune dysregulation enables autoreactive B cells to produce anti-nephrin autoantibodies targeting the slit diaphragm.
Relapse-associated circulating factors can induce podocyte CD80/B7-1 expression during active disease.
Immune and circulating permeability signals converge on the podocyte, destabilizing foot process architecture and slit diaphragm organization.
CD80 activation is associated with podocyte actin reorganization and subsequent foot process effacement.
Anti-nephrin antibodies directly disrupt slit diaphragm integrity, leading to podocyte foot process effacement.
Foot process and slit diaphragm disruption reduce barrier permselectivity, allowing albumin-predominant proteinuria.
Barrier failure produces heavy proteinuria with downstream hypoalbuminemia, edema, and dyslipidemia.

nodes.description: Anti-nephrin autoantibodies define an autoantibody-associated subgroup within acquired diffuse podocytopathies that includes many biopsy-proven MCD cases. In adult MCD/primary FSGS cohorts, seropositivity tracks with active nephrotic syndrome, more severe proteinuria, and relapse dynamics, supporting nephrin-directed immune injury in a subset rather than all MCD.

evidence.reference_title: Anti-nephrin antibodies in adult Chinese patients with minimal change disease and primary focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Anti-nephrin antibodies were detected in 43% of all patients, with 30% testing positive for anti-nephrin IgG, 26% for anti-nephrin IgM, and 13.1% for both antibodies.

evidence.reference_title: New insights into the biology and treatment of minimal change disease and focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.
evidence.snippet: Since the discovery of antinephrin antibodies and antibodies against other slit diaphragm components in a subset of patients with minimal change disease and focal segmental glomerulosclerosis, there has been a transformation of our understanding of disease pathogenesis and treatment rationale.

evidence.reference_title: The podocyte slit-diaphragm: target of anti-nephrin antibodies.
evidence.snippet: Insights from clinical studies and experimental models support the concept that pathogenic antibodies engaging the extracellular domain of nephrin disrupt the integrity of the slit-diaphragm and signal changes to the podocyte cytoskeleton that lead to foot process effacement and proteinuria.

evidence.reference_title: Autoantibodies Targeting Nephrin in Podocytopathies.
evidence.snippet: Experimental immunization induced a nephrotic syndrome, a minimal change disease-like phenotype, IgG localization to the podocyte slit diaphragm, nephrin phosphorylation, and severe cytoskeletal changes in mice.

nodes.description: Cyclosporine and tacrolimus are used as steroid-sparing agents for frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent MCD. Combined with reduced-dose prednisolone, they achieve similar remission rates with fewer steroid toxicities and may also stabilize the podocyte actin cytoskeleton.

evidence.reference_title: Interventions for minimal change disease in adults with nephrotic syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Compared with prednisolone alone, CNIs with reduced-dose prednisolone or without prednisolone probably make little or no difference to the number achieving complete remission

nodes.description: First-line treatment for MCD. Adult cohorts show high remission rates after corticosteroid induction, but frequent relapses drive use of steroid- sparing strategies in frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent disease.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical course of adult-onset minimal change disease.
evidence.snippet: After 16 weeks of corticosteroid treatment, 92 (88%) of these patients had reached remission.

evidence.reference_title: Minimal Change Disease.
evidence.snippet: The mainstay of therapy is prednisone, but steroid-sensitive forms frequently relapse and this leads to a percentage of patients requiring second-line steroid-sparing immunosuppression.

nodes.description: Alkylating immunosuppressive therapy used as a steroid-sparing option for frequently relapsing, steroid-dependent, or steroid-resistant adult MCD when relapse burden or steroid toxicity justifies escalation.

evidence.reference_title: Minimal-change disease in adolescents and adults: epidemiology and therapeutic response.
evidence.snippet: Twelve of 14 (85.71%) steroid-resistant cases had CR or PR after alternative immunosuppression with cyclophosphamide, or mycophenolate mofetil.

evidence.reference_title: Management of adult patients with podocytopathies: an update from the ERA Immunonephrology Working Group.
evidence.snippet: This article provides a perspective of the Immunonephrology Working Group (IWG) of the European Renal Association (ERA) and discusses the KDIGO 2021 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Glomerular Diseases focusing on the management of MCD and primary forms of FSGS in the context of recently published evidence, with a special emphasis on the role of rituximab, cyclophosphamide, supportive treatment options and ongoing clinical trials in the field.

nodes.description: Podocyte injury in MCD translates into loss of glomerular barrier integrity, driving selective proteinuria and the nephrotic state.

evidence.reference_title: Minimal Change Disease.
evidence.snippet: Although the cause is unknown and it is likely that different subgroups of disease recognize a different pathogenesis, immunologic dysregulation and modifications of the podocyte are thought to synergize in altering the integrity of the glomerular basement membrane and therefore determining proteinuria.

nodes.description: MCD is best framed as an immune-mediated podocytopathy in which adaptive immune dysregulation is implicated, but no single upstream circulating factor explains all cases. Human relapse cohorts show cytokine perturbations and urinary CD80 elevation, while review literature supports interacting T-cell, B-cell, and podocyte pathways rather than one definitive proximal mediator.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular Mechanisms of Proteinuria in Minimal Change Disease.
evidence.snippet: MCD has been traditionally thought to be mediated by an unknown circulating factor(s), probably released by T cells that directly target podocytes leading to podocyte ultrastructural changes and proteinuria.

evidence.reference_title: Usefulness of the cytokines expression of Th1/Th2/Th17 and urinary CD80 excretion in adult-onset minimal change disease.
evidence.snippet: The cytokines GM-CSF and TRANCE are increased and the urinary CD80 levels are elevated in adult-onset MCD patients in relapse, indicating a disorder of Th1/Th2/Th17 balance and that the elevated excretion of CD80 may underlie the pathogenesis and development of adult-onset MCD

evidence.reference_title: Molecular and Cellular Mechanisms for Proteinuria in Minimal Change Disease.
evidence.snippet: However, the hypothesis of a single cell subset or molecule as cause of MCD is not supported by research and an interactive process seems more logical.

nodes.description: Mycophenolate-based immunosuppression, including mycophenolate mofetil and enteric-coated mycophenolate sodium formulations, is used as a steroid- sparing option in frequently relapsing or steroid-dependent adult MCD and as salvage therapy in selected resistant cases.

evidence.reference_title: Efficacy of Mycophenolate in Steroid-Dependent and Frequently Relapsing Adult Minimal Change Disease: A Retrospective Cohort Study.
evidence.snippet: MF therapy was able to maintain steroid-free remission for the whole duration of therapy (12 months) in 20 (83.3%) patients.

evidence.reference_title: An open-label randomized controlled trial of low-dose corticosteroid plus enteric-coated mycophenolate sodium versus standard corticosteroid treatment for minimal change nephrotic syndrome in adults (MSN Study).
evidence.snippet: Thus, in adult patients, treatment with low-dose prednisone plus enteric-coated mycophenolate sodium was not superior to a standard high-dose prednisone regimen to induce complete remission of MCNS.

nodes.description: Massive proteinuria (>3.5 g/day in adults), hypoalbuminemia, edema, and hyperlipidemia constitute the classic presentation.

evidence.reference_title: Minimal Change Disease.
evidence.snippet: Minimal change disease (MCD) is a major cause of idiopathic nephrotic syndrome (NS), characterized by intense proteinuria leading to edema and intravascular volume depletion.

nodes.description: Glomerular barrier failure causes the classic nephrotic syndrome of heavy proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, and hyperlipidemia. Severe adult nephrosis can also be accompanied by acute kidney injury and thromboembolism.

evidence.reference_title: Minimal Change Disease.
evidence.snippet: Minimal change disease (MCD) is a major cause of idiopathic nephrotic syndrome (NS), characterized by intense proteinuria leading to edema and intravascular volume depletion.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical course of adult-onset minimal change disease.
evidence.snippet: Acute kidney injury was observed in 50 (40%) patients. Recovery of kidney function occurred almost without exception. Arterial or venous thrombosis occurred in 11 (9%) patients.

nodes.description: A relapse-associated circulating factor can induce podocyte CD80/B7-1 expression in MCD. This state is reflected by elevated urinary CD80 during active disease and is linked to actin cytoskeletal remodeling and slit diaphragm dysfunction rather than being a universally specific marker across all nephrotic syndromes.

evidence.reference_title: Serum from minimal change patients in relapse increases CD80 expression in cultured podocytes.
evidence.snippet: Sera from MCD patients in relapse, but not in remission, stimulated CD80 expression in cultured podocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Urinary CD80: a biomarker for a favorable response to corticosteroids in minimal change disease.
evidence.snippet: Some studies suggest that subjects with steroid-sensitive MCD may express CD80 in their podocytes during relapse and that this expression is associated with high urinary levels of CD80.

nodes.description: The hallmark of MCD is diffuse effacement of podocyte foot processes visible on electron microscopy, despite normal-appearing glomeruli on light microscopy. Podocyte injury involves disruption of the actin cytoskeleton, nephrin and slit diaphragm disorganization, and loss of podocyte architecture.

evidence.reference_title: Minimal Change Disease.
evidence.snippet: The pathologic hallmark of disease is absence of visible alterations by light microscopy and effacement of foot processes by electron microscopy.

nodes.description: Heavy proteinuria predominantly of albumin (selective proteinuria) is characteristic. Proteinuria typically exceeds 3.5 g/day in adults and resolves with steroid treatment in steroid-sensitive disease.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular Mechanisms of Proteinuria in Minimal Change Disease.
evidence.snippet: It is characterized by massive proteinuria, edema, hypoalbuminemia, and podocyte foot process effacement on electron microscopy.

nodes.description: Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody used for frequently relapsing or steroid- dependent MCD. Adult retrospective data show a 78% overall response rate, but relapse remains common and optimal dosing is still evolving.

evidence.reference_title: Rituximab for minimal-change nephrotic syndrome in adulthood: predictive factors for response, long-term outcomes and tolerance.
evidence.snippet: Complete (NS remission and withdrawal of all immunosuppressants) and partial (NS remission and withdrawal of at least one immunosuppressants) clinical responses were obtained for 25 and 7 patients, respectively (overall response 78%)
